title: Effects of Resistance Training Frequency on Measures of Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
population: Healthy adults in experimental resistance training studies
keyFinding: When weekly training volume is equated, training a muscle group about twice per week appears to produce better hypertrophy than once per week.
takeaway: If muscle growth is the goal, hitting a muscle more than once per week is usually a strong default.

title: Strength and Hypertrophy Adaptations Between Low- vs. High-Load Resistance Training: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
population: Adults in resistance training studies comparing low and high loads
keyFinding: Muscle hypertrophy was similar between low-load and high-load training protocols when sets were performed to momentary muscular failure, while heavy loads improved 1RM strength more.
takeaway: You do not need heavy loads for hypertrophy every time, but heavier work still matters if maximal strength is also a goal.

title: Influence of Resistance Training Proximity-to-Failure on Skeletal Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis
population: Healthy adults of varying ages and resistance training experience
keyFinding: The review found no strong evidence that training to momentary muscular failure is superior to non-failure training for hypertrophy.
takeaway: Sets probably need to be hard, but every set does not need to end in absolute failure to support muscle growth.

title: Effects of range of motion on muscle development during resistance training interventions: A systematic review
authors: Brad J Schoenfeld and Jozo Grgic
population: Adults in longitudinal resistance training studies
keyFinding: Full range of motion appeared to benefit lower-body hypertrophy compared with partial range of motion, while upper-body findings were more mixed.
takeaway: Using a full range of motion is a practical default, especially for lower-body exercises.

title: Resistance Training Volume Enhances Muscle Hypertrophy but Not Strength in Trained Men
keyFinding: Men performing higher training volumes increased muscle hypertrophy more than lower-volume groups, while strength gains were not clearly greater.
takeaway: Doing more hard sets can help hypertrophy in trained lifters, but adding volume has a clearer effect on size than on strength.

title: Resistance training prescription for muscle strength and hypertrophy in healthy adults: a systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis
type: Systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis
population: Healthy adults across randomized resistance training trials
keyFinding: All resistance training prescriptions outperformed non-exercise controls for hypertrophy, and higher-load multiset twice-weekly training ranked highest for hypertrophy.
takeaway: Hypertrophy can be built with many reasonable programs, but multiset training is a strong common denominator.

title: Effect of free-weight vs. machine-based strength training on maximal strength, hypertrophy and jump performance - a systematic review and meta-analysis
population: Adults aged 18 to 60 in studies comparing free weights and machines
keyFinding: No clear difference was found between free-weight and machine-based training for hypertrophy in direct comparisons.
takeaway: Exercise selection can be based on goals, comfort, and access because both free weights and machines can support muscle growth.

title: Resistance Training Volume Enhances Muscle Hypertrophy, but Not Strength in Postmenopausal Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: G Nascimento de Oliveira Junior et al.
keyFinding: A higher-volume protocol increased leg lean mass more than a lower-volume protocol, while strength gains were similar between the training groups.
takeaway: In older women, doing more sets may help hypertrophy more than strength when the exercises and effort level are otherwise similar.

title: Volume Load Rather Than Resting Interval Influences Muscle Hypertrophy During High-Intensity Resistance Training
population: Healthy adults performing unilateral knee extension training
keyFinding: Protocols that preserved more volume load produced greater quadriceps hypertrophy than shorter-rest conditions with lower volume load.
takeaway: Rest length matters mostly because it can change how much quality work you can complete.

title: Longer Interset Rest Periods Enhance Muscle Strength and Hypertrophy in Resistance-Trained Men
keyFinding: Longer rest intervals led to greater gains in strength and were associated with superior hypertrophy outcomes compared with shorter rests in trained men.
takeaway: If muscle growth stalls, longer rests can be useful because they may help you sustain performance across sets.

title: Influence of Resistance Training Exercise Order on Muscle Strength, Hypertrophy, and Anabolic Hormones in Older Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial
keyFinding: Both exercise orders improved strength and lean tissue, with no large hypertrophy advantage from starting with multi-joint or single-joint movements.
takeaway: Exercise order can be chosen around priorities and comfort because either order can still support progress.

title: Influence of resistance training load on measures of skeletal muscle hypertrophy and improvements in maximal strength and neuromuscular task performance: A systematic review and meta-analysis
population: Healthy adults across 45 resistance training studies
keyFinding: Higher- and lower-load training produced similar hypertrophy overall, while higher loads improved one-repetition maximum strength more.
takeaway: Loads can be flexible for hypertrophy, but heavier work remains useful when maximal strength is a goal.

title: Resistance Training Performed With Single and Multiple Sets Induces Similar Improvements in Muscular Strength, Muscle Mass, Muscle Quality, and IGF-1 in Older Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial
keyFinding: Single-set and multiple-set training both improved strength, lean soft tissue, muscle quality, and IGF-1 after 12 weeks in older women.
takeaway: Early in training, simple lower-volume programs can still work well, especially for beginners and older adults.

title: The Effectiveness of Frequency-Based Resistance Training Protocols on Muscular Performance and Hypertrophy in Trained Males: A Critically Appraised Topic
keyFinding: When volume is equated, lower-frequency and higher-frequency training both improved strength and hypertrophy, with only small differences between approaches.
takeaway: Training frequency can often be chosen based on schedule and recovery if weekly hard work is kept similar.

title: Partial Compared with Full Range of Motion Resistance Training for Muscle Hypertrophy: A Brief Review and an Identification of Potential Mechanisms
authors: Daniel E Newmire and Darryn S Willoughby
population: Healthy adults in resistance training research
keyFinding: The review suggested partial range of motion may provide some hypertrophy benefits in certain contexts, though evidence was limited and mixed.
takeaway: Full range of motion is still the safer default, but partials may have a place depending on the exercise and goal.

title: Low-Load Resistance Training to Volitional Failure Induces Muscle Hypertrophy Similar to Volume-Matched, Velocity Fatigue
population: Healthy adults performing low-load resistance training
keyFinding: Low-load training taken to volitional failure produced hypertrophy similar to a volume-matched protocol based on velocity fatigue.
takeaway: For lighter loads, pushing sets hard can still build muscle without relying on heavy weights.

title: Effects of Resistance Training Performed with Different Loads in Untrained and Trained Male Adult Individuals on Maximal Strength and Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review
population: Healthy adult men with both untrained and trained participants across included studies
keyFinding: The review found that both trained and untrained men can hypertrophy across a range of loads, although the literature was weighted toward untrained participants.
takeaway: Experience level changes how confidently you can generalize findings, so trained-lifter evidence should be interpreted more carefully.
